20: A revelation of God: the father of the prodigal son runs, embraces, and restores without condition. The father in the parable is transparently the image of God — no other first-century Jewish father would run (an undignified act) to embrace a disgraced son before any apology had been given. The parable is Jesus's defense of his own practice (receiving sinners) by revealing the Father's character: God himself is the running father who hosts the party for the found son and pleads with the older brother. Every feature of the father's excess (running, robe, ring, sandals, feast, self-humiliation at the party's edge) is a portrait of divine grace preceding, enabling, and exceeding human repentance.
1: 'Now the tax collectors and sinners were all drawing near to hear him' — the gathering of tax collectors and sinners around Jesus is the enacted answer to the question the chapter's three parables will address: why does Christ eat with sinners? Their drawing near (ēggizon — they were coming close) is the movement toward Christ that every sinner must make. They come to hear — and hearing, they find the seeking-finding-feasting Father. Christ's attraction for the socially excluded is not despite his holiness but because of it: the Holy One of God is also the Savior of sinners (1 Timothy 1:15).
2: 'And the Pharisees and the scribes grumbled, saying, This man receives sinners and eats with them' — the Pharisaic grumbling (diagongyzō — the LXX word for Israel's wilderness murmuring, Luke 5:30; Luke 19:7) is the occasion for the three parables. Their complaint is accurate: Jesus does receive sinners and eat with them. Their error is in treating this as a scandal. Christ's eating with sinners is not a compromise of his holiness but the expression of the Father's character: the God who pursues the lost, rejoices at their return, and throws a party for the found. The Pharisees grumble; the Father celebrates.
3: The three parables of Luke 15 (lost sheep, lost coin, lost son) form the Gospel's most sustained account of the Father's heart toward the lost. They are Christ's theological self-defense: this is why I eat with sinners — because this is what the Father is like, and I am doing what the Father does. The parables are not merely ethical teachings about generosity; they are Christological declarations about the divine mission of seeking and saving. Christ is the seeking Shepherd (Luke 15:4-7), and his entire Incarnation is the going-after of the one lost sheep.
4: 'What man of you, having a hundred sheep, if he has lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the open country, and go after the one that is lost, until he finds it?' — the Good Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine for the one is Christ himself (John 10:11-16 — 'I am the good shepherd... I lay down my life for the sheep'). The one lost sheep is the entire human race — lost from God through the Fall. The Incarnation is the shepherd's leaving the fold (heaven) to go into the open country (fallen creation) to seek the lost. The cross is the rescue; the resurrection is the finding.
5: 'And when he has found it, he lays it on his shoulders, rejoicing' — the shepherd who lays the found sheep on his shoulders is the image of Christ carrying the cross (Luke 23:26; John 19:17) and also of the image of Christ bearing his people (Isaiah 46:4 — 'I will carry you'). The rejoicing (chairōn) of the shepherd is the 'joy set before him' (Hebrews 12:2) for which Christ endured the cross — the eschatological joy of recovering the lost. The shepherd's shoulders are the cross; the sheep laid across those shoulders is the redeemed humanity; the rejoicing is the resurrection-joy of completed redemption.
6: 'And when he comes home, he calls together his friends and his neighbors, saying to them, Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost' — the communal celebration over the found sheep previews the resurrection-joy that Christ's recovery of lost humanity produces in heaven. 'Rejoice with me' is the invitation to share in the Shepherd's own joy — the joy of the triune God over the salvation of sinners. This is the eschatological party that the Father throws (Luke 15:22-24 in the prodigal son), the wedding supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7-9), the final gathering that every saving event anticipates.
7: 'Just so, I tell you, there will be more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance' — the disproportionate joy over the one repentant sinner is the measure of how much God values the recovery of the lost. The 'ninety-nine righteous who need no repentance' is not a real category (all have sinned, Romans 3:23) but the self-righteous category that the grumbling Pharisees occupy in their own minds. The joy of heaven — shared by Father, Son, Spirit, and angels (Luke 15:10) — is the measure of the value of one saved soul.
8: 'Or what woman, having ten silver coins, if she loses one coin, does not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek diligently until she finds it?' — the second parable of seeking uses a woman seeking a lost coin as the image of divine diligence in recovering what is lost. The lamp she lights is the light of the gospel (John 1:9 — Christ as the true light; Luke 11:33-36 — the lamp that illuminates the whole house). The thorough searching (katarousan — sweeping carefully) is the thoroughness of the divine search for the lost — the Incarnation leaving no corner of human experience unexplored.
9: 'And when she has found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, Rejoice with me, for I have found the coin that I had lost' — the third instance of communal joy-in-recovery prepares for the full communal celebration of the prodigal son's return (Luke 15:22-32). The pattern is consistent: finding → communal joy. Heaven's joy at a sinner's return (Luke 15:10) is not private or quiet; it is the communal celebration of all who love the Seeker. The coin's finding is celebrated by the whole neighborhood; the sinner's finding is celebrated by the whole company of heaven.
10: 'Just so, I tell you, there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents' — the angelic joy at human repentance is the joy of those who have been waiting and watching the divine redemption-drama unfold. The angels who announced Christ's birth (Luke 2:10-14 — 'great joy'), who ministered to him in Gethsemane (Luke 22:43), and who announced his resurrection (Luke 24:4-7) are also those who celebrate every sinner's return. The recovery of one lost soul is an event of universal cosmic significance — the continuation of the great redemption-drama that began with the Incarnation.
11: 'And he said, There was a man who had two sons' — the third and greatest parable of Luke 15 introduces the prodigal son. The two sons represent two ways of being lost: the younger (far from home, conscious of sin) and the elder (near home, unconscious of his own lostness). Christ comes for both — and the parable ends with the Father's arms open to both. The two sons together represent the totality of human alienation from God: explicit rebellion (younger) and religious self-righteousness (elder), both needing the Father's welcome.
12: 'Father, give me the share of property that is coming to me' — the younger son's demand for his inheritance while his father is living is an act of social death: symbolically wishing the father dead in order to receive what a dead father would give. This is the image of human sin against the Father: the creation demands from God the benefits of his generosity while treating him as dead — pushing him aside as irrelevant while using his gifts. The Incarnation is the Father's response: not punitive withdrawal but self-giving love (John 3:16 — 'he gave his only Son').
13: 'Not many days later, the younger son gathered all he had and took a journey into a far country, and there he squandered his property in reckless living' — the far country (chōran makran) is the alienation of sin from the Father. The 'recklessness' (asōtōs — prodigally, without saving) is the misuse of the good gifts the Father gave. The created order is the Father's gift to humanity (Gen 1-2); the misuse and exploitation of creation is the prodigal living in the far country. Christ comes from the Father's presence into this far country (Philippians 2:7 — 'coming in human likeness') to bring the prodigal home.
14: 'And when he had spent everything, a severe famine arose in that country, and he began to be in need' — the famine that empties the far country and brings the son to destitution is the natural consequence of the life without the Father. Apart from the Father, every resource is finite; every pleasure fades; every abundance runs out. The son who demanded everything from the father has nothing when the father's gifts are exhausted. This is the portrait of fallen humanity without God: finite resources, growing need, no recourse. The gospel begins at the moment of 'he began to be in need.'
15: 'So he went and hired himself out to one of the citizens of that country, who sent him into his fields to feed pigs' — the Israelite son feeding pigs (ritually impure animals, Leviticus 11:7) in a Gentile land is the image of complete degradation: covenant people serving idols, the image of God reduced to pig-keeping. This is the portrait of Israel in exile — and of all humanity in the far country of sin: made for communion with the Father, reduced to feeding swine. The 'citizen of that country' to whom he is enslaved is the image of every power that exploits the human being separated from God.
16: 'And he was longing to be fed with the pods that the pigs ate, and no one gave him anything' — the son's longing for pig-food and the giving of 'nothing' by anyone pictures the complete abandonment of the far country. In the Father's house there is 'enough and to spare' (Luke 15:17); in the far country, not even pig food satisfies. The hunger of the far country is the hunger that only the Father can fill — the restlessness Augustine described: 'our heart is restless, until it finds its rest in Thee.' The feeding of the five thousand (Luke 9:17 — all ate and were satisfied) is Christ's provision for those who have been 'given nothing' by the world.
17: 'But when he came to himself, he said, How many of my father's hired servants have more than enough bread, but I perish here with hunger!' — 'coming to himself' (eis heauton elthōn — coming into himself) is the moment of repentance's beginning: the recovery of sanity, the recognition of where one truly is. The prodigal's self-recognition (I am perishing; my father's house has abundance) is the first movement of repentance. This coming-to-oneself is the work of the Spirit (John 16:8 — 'when he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin'). Repentance is not self-generated but Spirit-enabled.
18: 'I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you' — the prodigal's prepared confession ('sinned against heaven and before you') acknowledges two dimensions of sin: the vertical (against God/heaven) and the horizontal (before the father's face). This is David's double-confession in Psalms 51:4 — 'Against you, you only, have I sinned, and done what is evil in your sight.' The prodigal's recognition that his sin is first against God, not merely against himself or social convention, is the beginning of genuine repentance rather than mere regret.
19: 'I am no longer worthy to be called your son. Treat me as one of your hired servants' — the prodigal's self-assessment of unworthiness is accurate (he has forfeited his sonship by his actions) but his proposed solution (hired servant) underestimates the Father's grace. He can imagine being taken back as a servant; he cannot imagine being received as a son. The Father's response will exceed every expectation: not hired servant but fully restored son, clothed, ringed, feasted. This is the pattern of grace: the sinner's self-estimation is accurate about sin but radically underestimates the Father's love.
21: 'And the son said to him, Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son' — the prodigal's prepared speech is interrupted before completion: the father does not wait to hear the 'treat me as a hired servant' proposal. The interruption is itself the grace — the father's actions (robe, ring, sandals, feast) preempt and exceed the son's request. Christ does not wait for the sinner to finish the penitential speech before the forgiveness is given: on the cross, 'Father, forgive them' (Luke 23:34) is spoken before any request comes from the crucifiers.
22: 'But the father said to his servants, Bring quickly the best robe, and put it on him, and put a ring on his hand, and shoes on his feet' — the restoration items are the marks of full restored sonship: the best robe (covering the shame of the far-country rags — cf. Christ clothing the naked; Isaiah 61:10 — 'he has clothed me with garments of salvation'), the ring (signet ring — restored authority), and sandals (slaves went barefoot; sonship restores shoes). The Father's rapid clothing of the returning prodigal is the Incarnation-pattern: Christ covers human shame with his own righteousness (2 Corinthians 5:21 — 'he made him to be sin... so that in him we might become the righteousness of God').
23: 'And bring the fattened calf and kill it, and let us eat and celebrate' — the feast (euophranthōmen — let us be merry/rejoice) for the returning prodigal is the eschatological feast of the kingdom (Isaiah 25:6; Revelation 19:7-9 — the wedding supper of the Lamb). The fattened calf killed for the celebration echoes the Passover sacrifice and foreshadows the cross: the Father's feast for returning sinners is made possible by the death of the Son, who is himself the sacrificial lamb (John 1:29 — 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world'). The feast flows from the sacrifice.
24: 'For this my son was dead, and is alive again; he was lost, and is found' — the double reversal (dead-to-alive, lost-to-found) is the resurrection-pattern stated in explicit terms. The prodigal was spiritually dead (Ephesians 2:1 — 'dead in trespasses and sins') and has been made alive (Ephesians 2:5 — 'made us alive together with Christ'). He was lost (Luke 9:10 — 'the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost') and is found. The father's declaration over the returning prodigal is the Father's declaration over every returning sinner — and its grammar is the grammar of resurrection: was dead, is alive; was lost, is found.
25: 'Now his elder son was in the field, and as he came and drew near to the house, he heard music and dancing' — the elder son outside the house while the celebration is inside creates the spatial image of the self-righteous who remain outside the feast. He has 'been here all the time' (Luke 15:29) — geographically near the father but spiritually distant. The music and dancing he hears from outside is the sound of the kingdom's celebration that he refuses to join. The elder son represents those who are 'almost in' — near the father but without the father's heart.
26: 'And he called one of the servants and asked what these things meant' — the elder son's need to ask about what is happening in his own father's house reveals the depth of his alienation. He has been near the father for years without knowing the father's heart. The Pharisees who grumbled (Luke 15:2) are in exactly this position: they know the law, they perform its requirements, but they do not know the father who gives the law — they do not know that the father runs, embraces, and feasts. Near in observance, far in heart.
27: 'And he said to him, Your brother has come, and your father has killed the fattened calf, because he has received him back safe and sound' — the servant's report describes the father's actions without their internal motivation. The elder son receives only the external facts: the return, the calf-killing, the reception. He does not receive the internal reality: the father's love, the resurrection-joy, the grace. The external facts without the internal motivation produce anger rather than celebration. This is the Pharisees' position: they see Jesus's actions (eating with sinners) without understanding the Father's heart (seeking and saving the lost).
28: 'But he was angry and refused to go in. His father came out and entreated him' — the elder son's anger and refusal to enter is the Pharisees' anger at the gospel's inclusiveness. But the father comes out — again (he ran toward the prodigal, Luke 15:20; he comes out to the elder son, Luke 15:28). The Father pursues both sons. This is the shape of the divine love: it does not wait inside for the lost to come; it goes out after both the far-country rebel and the near-home moralist. The elder son's exclusion from the feast is self-chosen; the father comes out to invite him in.
29: 'Look, these many years I have been serving you, and I never disobeyed your command, yet you never gave me a young goat, that I might celebrate with my friends' — the elder son's complaint reveals his fundamental misunderstanding: he has been treating the father's household as an employer-employee relationship rather than a father-son relationship. He counts years, counts commands obeyed, counts goats not given. He has been in the father's house without access to the father's heart — a description of legal religion without the Spirit, of service without sonship (Galatians 4:1-7).
30: 'But when this son of yours came, who has devoured your property with prostitutes, you killed the fattened calf for him!' — the elder son's 'this son of yours' (not 'my brother') distances himself from both the prodigal and the father. His bitterness is the bitterness of those who have worked without understanding grace — who have kept accounts and find the grace-economy unfair. Christ's answer to this complaint is not explanation but invitation: 'Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours' (Luke 15:31). The elder son had everything and did not know it, because he was counting goats instead of enjoying the father.
31: 'Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours' — the father's gentle address ('Son' — teknon, child) and the declaration of the elder son's permanent access to all the father has is the corrective to his servant-mentality. The elder son has been treating as an employee what he has as a son. This is the believer's position: 'if children, then heirs — heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ' (Romans 8:17). The elder son already has everything the Father has — but joy in it requires receiving the Father's heart, not merely occupying the Father's house.
32: 'It was fitting to celebrate and be glad, for this your brother was dead, and is alive; he was lost, and is found' — the parable ends with the father's invitation standing and the elder son's response unresolved. The dead-alive, lost-found reversal is restated for the elder son: it is fitting (edei — divine necessity) to rejoice, because this is what the resurrection looks like. The parable ends open: will the elder son enter the feast? Luke leaves the question open for the Pharisees — will they accept the justification of sinners and enter the kingdom's joy? The invitation stands.
